title: Arrays - a row of values, and a pointer in disguise
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: A C array is a fixed run of same-type values, indexed from 0. The array's name acts like a pointer to its first element.
explanation: int a[3] reserves three ints in a row. a[0] is the first. The quiet truth of C: the name a is essentially the address of a[0], which is why arrays and pointers feel so tangled here - a[i] is really *(a + i). Indexing is fast, but C won't stop you from reading a[5] of a 3-slot array; staying in bounds is your job.

exercises.question: The name of an array, used on its own, acts like...
options: a pointer to its first element
hints: a is the address of a[0].
A pointer to the first element.

exercises.question:
C arrays count positions starting from which number?
